← History & Culture
Guanacaste · Costa Rica · Greater Nicoya

Chorotega Place Names

Landmarks, Towns & Geographical Features of Guanacaste
24Place Names
4Cultural Periods
690Words Survive

The Chorotega — whose name, from Nahuatl, means "people surrounded by enemies" — were the dominant indigenous people of Guanacaste and the Nicoya Peninsula at the time of the Spanish conquest. They were not native to the region: the evidence agrees that they migrated south from central Mexico, likely from the area of Cholula, between the eighth and thirteenth centuries A.D., settling what archaeologists call the Greater Nicoya subarea — a cultural zone spanning northwestern Costa Rica and Pacific Nicaragua.

At their height the Chorotega lived in plaza towns of up to 20,000 people, governed by elected councils of elders and chiefs. Corn, beans, squash, cacao, meat, and fish were dietary staples, and cacao beans served as currency — counterfeited, the Spanish chroniclers noted, by emptying the shells and refilling them with dirt. They were accomplished astronomers and potters, producing the polychrome ceramics that became prestige trade goods across Mesoamerica and the Intermediate Area. Their language, Chorotega or Mangue — a member of the Oto-Manguean family — was extinct in Costa Rica by the eighteenth century; only around 690 words survive in the documented record.

Archaeologists divide Greater Nicoya history into four ceramic periods: Tempisque (500 B.C.–A.D. 300, pre-Chorotega), Bagaces (A.D. 300–800, the Chorotega's arrival), Sapoá (A.D. 800–1350, the cultural apogee — the great polychromes, the trade settlements of Bahía Culebra, and iconographic ties to the Mixtec calendar and Quetzalcoatl), and Ometepe (A.D. 1350–1550, ending with the Spanish conquest). Recent University of Calgary excavations have revised this picture: sites once attributed to the later-arriving Nicarao were in fact Chorotega work, pushing the Chorotega achievement earlier and further than previously understood.

What survives today is fragmentary but tenacious: the pottery tradition of Guaitil and Nambi, the place names catalogued below, and a handful of words — among them the names of rivers, towns, chiefs, and princesses — embedded in the landscape of a province that still bears, as its first word, a name the Chorotega gave to a tree.

The Nicoya Complex Chiefs & Princesses Settlements & Living Craft Trees, Vessels & Land The North & the River
Distribution
Chorotega Place Names in Modern Guanacaste
Fifteen Chorotega and Nahuatl-derived place names plotted on the modern map of Guanacaste and the Nicoya Peninsula. The Río Tempisque runs the length of the province as its hydrological axis; the concentration of names on the peninsula corresponds to the Greater Nicoya archaeological heartland. Map data: OpenStreetMap contributors / Natural Earth.
I
The Nicoya Complex
Guanacaste
Means "ear tree," named for the seeds of the Guanacaste tree, which resemble a human ear. Both the province and Costa Rica's national tree carry this Chorotega name.
Nicoya — the City
The colonial city built directly on the ceremonial plaza of the Chorotega settlement of Chief Nicoa, the most powerful Chorotega ruler of the sixteenth century. Gil González Dávila's expedition arrived overland from the south in 1522–1523, having landed at a bay they named San Vicente — present-day Caldera — and marched north through the interior. When the Spaniards entered the town, Chief Nicoa gave González Dávila gold worth 14,000 castellanos and permitted himself and reportedly 6,000 subjects to be baptised. The expedition treasurer Cereceda counted 6,063 inhabitants under Nicoa's leadership — nearly five and a half times larger than the next biggest settlement encountered along the Pacific coast.
The received account of a peaceful diplomatic encounter omits the ending: González reported that the inhabitants were converted to the Catholic faith before Nicoa was killed and large numbers of his people enslaved for export. The Church of San Blas, established 1522–1544 and rebuilt in calicanto masonry in 1644, stands on the site of the Chorotega ceremonial plaza described by the chronicler Oviedo in 1529. A corn plant — the sacred crop of the Chorotega — is carved into the church facade. In 2012, 154 burials dating from 1600 to 1813 were discovered beneath the church floor.
An alternative etymology from Nahuatl, Necoc Yáotl, means "with enemies on both sides" — a description that, in the end, proved accurate. Today Nicoya is the canton capital, population approximately 17,000.
Nicoya — the Peninsula
The long finger of land extending south into the Pacific, forming the Gulf of Nicoya to the east. The heartland of Chorotega culture in Costa Rica, and home to most of the places in this catalogue: Guaitil, Nambi, Matambú, Nosara, Nandayure, Diriá, and the living Chorotega pottery tradition. The peninsula is also one of only five designated Blue Zones in the world — places identified by researcher Dan Buettner where people measurably live longer, healthier lives than the global average.
The factors specific to Nicoya include exceptionally calcium- and magnesium-rich water from the local geology, the persistence of the traditional Chorotega diet of corn, beans, and squash in rural communities, continued physical activity into old age, and strong social networks. The beans that Nambi is named for, the corn carved on the facade of San Blas, the squash grown alongside them in the milpa for three thousand years — the Chorotega diet that sustained the builders of Nacascolo is now studied by epidemiologists as a model for human longevity.
Greater Nicoya
The archaeological and cultural subarea defined by researchers, spanning northwestern Costa Rica and Pacific Nicaragua — the territory within which the Chorotega and related cultures developed their ceramic traditions, trade networks, and political structures from roughly 500 B.C. to the Spanish conquest. It is the framework within which Nacascolo, the polychrome wares (Papagayo, Pataky, and Granada polychromes), and the four chronological periods all make sense as one coherent cultural world.
Three distinct things — a city, a peninsula, an archaeological subarea — all bearing the name of one man, Chief Nicoa, who met the Spanish in 1523.
Gulf of Nicoya
Costa Rica's largest gulf — a funnel-shaped tectonic estuary roughly 80 km long and up to 50 km wide, bordered by the Nicoya Peninsula and fed at its head by the Río Tempisque. It covers 1,530 km², fringed by mangroves that serve as nurseries, nesting grounds, and buffers against coastal erosion, and is the country's most important fishing ground and the site of its main Pacific ports — the same waters from which the Chorotega drew fish as a dietary staple for over a thousand years.
By the early 1990s the gulf was already identified as overexploited by shrimp trawlers and the longline fleet; research through 2015 shows further deterioration, with most shrimp, eel, and catfish species in decline, compounded by urbanisation, tourism development, and agricultural runoff. A fourth dimension to a name that began with a chief in 1523: a city, a peninsula, an archaeological subarea, and now the most ecologically pressured body of water in the country.
II
Chiefs & Princesses
Nandayure
Named after a Chorotega princess. Her name combines nanda (stream) and yure (snake, flower, or bird) — "stream of the snake, flower, or bird." She was said to be the daughter of a powerful rival chief, and a tragic figure in Chorotega legend. Her name survives as a canton of Guanacaste.
Nosara
Named after a Chorotega princess, daughter of Chief Nicoa. Thought to derive from Chorotega roots loosely translated as "you're not going anywhere." It survives today as both a river and a well-known beach and village.
Diriá
Named after a famous Chorotega chief, and classified as a Chorotega toponym by linguists based on geographic distribution, characteristic endings, and historical sources. Survives today as a district in Santa Cruz canton and as a river.
Curime
Named after a Chorotega chief and high governor. The suffix -ime is said to indicate "abundance." Survives today as a barrio in Nicoya district.
III
Settlements & Living Craft
Papagayo
A former Chorotega settlement whose name persists today in the Gulf of Papagayo and the Papagayo Peninsula.
Pocosí
Another former Chorotega settlement whose name has survived into the modern era as a place name in the region.
Playa Nacascolo
A beach on the Papagayo Peninsula, named for Nacaome, one of the pre-Columbian Chorotega towns recorded before the conquest. The site (designated G-89 Na) was an important, long-inhabited settlement on Bahía Culebra, occupied roughly A.D. 300–1550, and a significant trading port between the Intermediate Area and Mesoamerica. Excavations began in the 1960s and continued through the 1980s; the site was extensively looted by huaqueros over more than a century, its polychrome ceramics prized by collectors worldwide.
The beach now sits within the Papagayo Peninsula Tourism Development Project — a 1990s initiative of the Instituto Costarricense de Turismo granting long-term concessions on public coastal land to international resort operators, including Four Seasons and Secrets. Costa Rican law technically guarantees public beach access, but in practice Nacascolo is accessible mainly to resort guests. The huaqueros took the objects; the resort took the beach. The Chorotega named it, the Spanish recorded it, the archaeologists studied it, the looters emptied it, the resort fenced it — and the name outlasted all of them. The community with the most legitimate ancestral connection to the land, the Chorotega descendants at Matambú a short drive away, had no seat at any of those tables.
Guaitil
A Chorotega village named for the local dye-giving Guaitil tree, between Santa Cruz and Nicoya. It remains a living centre of pre-Columbian pottery, where families hand-coil and fire vessels using techniques and pigments — terracotta, black, and white — passed down for over a thousand years. The authentic palette comes from local materials: the red and buff of the clay itself, black from ground stone, white from a chalky mineral.
Nambi
A locality on Route 21 just before Nicoya, home to a modest pottery workshop alongside hand-bound brooms in two sizes — short-handled for indoor floors, long-handled for clearing terracotta roofs. The word carries remarkable range: it was the name of a powerful Chorotega chieftain of Nicoya; in Costa Rica it names a drought-resistant bean variety, a staple of the Chorotega diet; and in the Río de la Plata region of South America it refers to a horse or mare with a drooping ear. A word that travelled, adapted, and survived across two continents.
Matambú
The only officially recognised Chorotega indigenous reserve in Costa Rica, established in 1980, in the Hojancha and Nicoya cantons. The name's etymology is not clearly documented — one of many words whose meaning the extinction of the Chorotega language took with it. Matambú is the most complete human survival of the Chorotega world today: while the language is gone and material culture largely absorbed into mainstream Guanacastecan life, living ceremonial practice persists in the temescal, a traditional steam ceremony in which each stake driven into the ground is accompanied by prayer, leaves are burned, and the community begins by apologising to Mother Earth for any harm.
A traditional farewell greeting also survives, fingers intertwining in a design resembling a snail shell — the spiral of infinity, friendship that never ends. The community's name for it is In Lak'Ech: I am the Other You. The reply is Hala Ken: You are the Other Me. The pottery of Guaitil and Nambi is the most visible material survival of the Chorotega; Matambú is the most complete, if fragile, human one.
Playa Ocotal
A beach just south of Playas del Coco on the Gulf of Papagayo, hemmed in by cliffs and dry forest. The name derives from the Nahuatl ocotl (pine resin or pine tree) with the Spanish suffix -al indicating abundance — "place of pines." The ocote pine (Pinus oocarpa) was not merely a tree but technology: its resin provided the torches that lit pre-Columbian Mesoamerica at night. Ocotl appears in place names all the way from central Mexico down through the Chorotega migration corridor into Guanacaste — carried south with the people who depended on it.
Huacas
A village in Carrillo canton, on the road between Filadelfia and Tamarindo, in the heart of former Chorotega coastal territory. The name derives from the Quechua wak'a — a sacred object, place, or burial site, where the spiritual world was understood to intersect with the physical. The word spread across Latin America during and after the colonial period as a general term for pre-Columbian sacred and burial sites, and was adopted by the huaqueros — grave robbers — as their professional descriptor. The word itself is the definition: a place set apart, a place where something was buried, a place once considered sacred.
IV
Trees, Vessels & the Land
Tepayac
A place name found in Guanacaste, from Classical Nahuatl Tepeyacac — tepetl ("hill"), yacatl ("nose" or "point"), and the locative -c — "at the nose of the mountain," describing the first and most prominent hill in a series of ridges. The word is most celebrated as the hill in present-day Mexico City where a pre-Hispanic shrine to the mother goddess Tonantzin already stood, and where in 1531 the Virgin of Guadalupe is said to have appeared to Juan Diego — the same pattern as San Blas in Nicoya, Christian sacred space superimposed on indigenous sacred ground. That Tepayac appears in Guanacaste reflects the Chorotega migration: they carried this Nahuatl topographic vocabulary south, applying it to hills and ridges that resembled the landscape they had left behind.
Guapinol
A Nahuatl name meaning "flour tree," applied to Hymenaea courbaril, one of the great trees of the Guanacaste dry forest, for the floury, edible pulp inside its seed pods. Several localities, rivers, and rural districts across Guanacaste carry the name. The tree was central to pre-Columbian Mesoamerican life: its pulp was ground with corn, peanuts, and cacao for a ceremonial drink; its resin, a form of copal, was burned as incense; its timber was prized for construction. The trees of Santa Rosa National Park, shaped by dry forest conditions, are characteristically shorter and more gnarled than those further south — the same tree, shaped by the same landscape that shaped the Chorotega themselves.
Guacalito
A place name found in Guanacaste and along the Gulf of Nicoya, from Classical Nahuatl huacalli (a wooden crate or vessel) with the Spanish diminutive -ito — "the little gourd." A guacal is a calabash gourd (Crescentia cujete) used since ancient times as a drinking vessel, food container, and water carrier. The gourd runs deep through pre-Columbian Mesoamerica: the sacred cuauhxicalli, the eagle-gourd bowl of the Aztecs used to hold ritual offerings, shares the same Nahuatl root. From the ceremonial to the domestic, Guacalito preserves that memory in the landscape.
Cuajiniquil
A district in Santa Cruz canton on the peninsula, and a settlement in La Cruz canton in the far north. Of Nahuatl origin, from icxitlcuilli — icxitl ("foot") and cuil ("to bend") — "tree of the crooked foot." It names Inga flexuosa, a leguminous tree with long edible seed pods of thirty centimetres or more. Found throughout Costa Rica, Nicaragua, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, and Panama, the tree's edible pods, considerable shade, and presence along watercourses made it a reliable landmark — the same naming logic as Guaitil: a place defined by the tree that grew there.
V
The North & the River
Curubandé
A district of Liberia canton, considered likely of Chorotega origin by linguists, though its precise etymological meaning remains uncertain.
Volcán Orosí
A stratovolcano in the Cordillera de Guanacaste near the Nicaragua border, within the UNESCO World Heritage Guanacaste National Park. Orosí is of Chorotega origin — the name of an indigenous king of the region. Recent linguistic research suggests the Chorotega language was tri-vocalic and may not have included the letter o, raising the possibility the original name was Urusí rather than Orosí. The first written record under this name dates to 1745.

A popular folk etymology holds that a priest climbing the volcano heard a mysterious voice reply to his speculation about silver mines with "Plata no, oro sí" — "Silver no, gold yes." But the Chorotega king and the linguistic evidence predate the priest by centuries. The volcano last erupted roughly 3,500 years ago.

Río Tempisque
The major river running through Guanacaste, considered to share its Nahuatl/Chorotega linguistic roots alongside names like Guanacaste and Nicoya. It runs the length of the province as its hydrological axis, feeding the Gulf of Nicoya at its head.

The Chorotega spoke Mangue, a member of the Oto-Manguean family. Many place names blend Chorotega cultural identity with Nahuatl linguistic roots; some, like Nicoya, are documented in Spanish colonial records, while others rest partly on oral tradition. The language itself was extinct in Costa Rica by the eighteenth century.

Notes & Sources

  1. On the Chorotega migration from Mexico and the Greater Nicoya subarea: McCafferty, G. & Steinbrenner, L. (eds.), The Archaeology of Greater Nicoya, University Press of Colorado, 2022; Constenla Umaña, A., "Las lenguas de la Gran Nicoya," Vínculos 18–19, 1994.
  2. On the Chorotega language (Mangue) and the surviving ~690 words: Quirós Rodríguez, J.S., Diccionario Español-Chorotega/Chorotega-Español, Universidad de Costa Rica, 2002.
  3. On Chorotega place names (Nicoya, Nandayure, Nosara, Diriá, Curime, Papagayo, Pocosí, Curubandé, Tempisque): Quirós Rodríguez, J.S., "Términos chorotegas en la toponimia guanacasteca," Letras 15–17, 1987, Universidad Nacional de Costa Rica.
  4. On cacao as currency and Chorotega economic life: Fernández de Oviedo y Valdés, G., Historia General y Natural de las Indias (1526–1535); tamarindohomepage.com, "Chorotegas: The First Guanacastecans."
  5. On the four archaeological periods and the University of Calgary revisions: McCafferty & Steinbrenner, op. cit.; McCafferty & Steinbrenner, "Chronological Implications for Greater Nicoya from the Santa Isabel Project, Nicaragua," 2005.
  6. On the Nicoya population count at contact: Cereceda, A. de, expedition treasurer, 1523, cited in "Nicoya (canton)," Wikipedia.
  7. On the Church of San Blas and the Chorotega ceremonial plaza: Rojas, M., "San Blas Museum, the colonial city's best kept secret," Voz de Guanacaste, Feb. 2023; "Archaeology and Ethnohistory on the Spanish Colonial Periphery," Historical Archaeology, Springer Nature, 2016.
  8. On the carved corn plant and the 2012 burials: Rojas, op. cit.; sensorialsunsets.com, "The Church of San Blas: The oldest church in Costa Rica."
  9. On the Nacascolo site (G-89 Na), occupation dates, and looting: Accola, R.M., "Sitio Nacascolo: Arqueología en un sitio saqueado," 1980; Wallace, H. & Accola, R., "Investigaciones arqueológicas preliminares de Nacascolo," Vínculos 6, 1980.
  10. On Nambi as a chieftain's name and bean variety: "Chiefs and Princesses from Guanacaste's History," Voz de Guanacaste, June 2018.
  11. On Guaitil and the pottery tradition's authentic palette: "Discovering the Indigenous Chorotega Culture," Costa Rica Star News, 2013; Instituto Clodomiro Picado, UCR.
  12. On Volcán Orosí and the possible original form Urusí: "Volcán Orosí," Wikipedia (Spanish); Fernández Bonilla, L., Historia de Costa Rica durante la dominación española; Quirós Rodríguez, op. cit.
  13. On Cuajiniquil (Inga flexuosa) and its Nahuatl etymology: "Jinicuil," wordmeaning.org; "Inga flexuosa," Wikipedia.
  14. On Guacalito and Nahuatl huacalli: "Guácala," Omniglot Blog; "Cuauhxicalli," Wikipedia.
  15. On Playa Ocotal and Nahuatl ocotl: "Ocotal," Wikipedia; general Mesoamerican ethnobotanical sources.
  16. On Huacas (wak'a) and the huaquero tradition: standard usage across Latin American archaeological literature; Accola, op. cit.
